children.title: Analyzing Movie Ratings and Runtime Trends
children.summary: The discussion covers visualizing movie ratings over time, particularly for action movies, highlighting the calculation of median ratings, first quartile, and third quartile for each year to enhance data understanding. SQL queries are recommended for summarizing data and creating visualizations in a data frame. Additionally, the relationship between movie runtime and ratings is explored by plotting duration against ratings to identify trends, with regression analysis suggested due to the absence of a linear trend. The need for feature engineering is noted, along with the creation of a heat map to visualize average ratings for specific runtimes. The speaker plans to analyze the trend of median ratings as runtime increases and fit a linear regression to assess potential trends, emphasizing the utility of SQL for handling large datasets.

children.title: Visualizing Movie Ratings Over Time
children.summary: The discussion focuses on analyzing the trend of movie ratings over the years, specifically for action movies. The speaker emphasizes the importance of calculating median ratings, first quartile, and third quartile for each year to understand the data better. They suggest using SQL queries to summarize the data and create visualizations, which can be effectively handled in a data frame.

table_name1 AS (

).

prolific_actors AS (

SELECT ...

)

table_name1,

children.title: Analyzing Movie Runtime and Ratings
children.summary: The discussion focuses on exploring the relationship between the runtime of action movies and their ratings. The approach involves plotting the movie duration against ratings to identify trends, suggesting that regression analysis may be appropriate. The speaker mentions the need for feature engineering due to the lack of a linear trend in the data. A heat map could be created to visualize the average ratings for specific runtimes. Additionally, the speaker plans to analyze the trend of median ratings as runtime increases and intends to fit a linear regression to the plot to assess any potential trend. The importance of using SQL for running large queries on datasets is also highlighted.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So everything all together. What if I go ahead and say that, can I understand whether the runtime of action movies influence the rating of the movie? So what I'm going to do is I'm going to actually plot on the X-axis the duration of the movie with respect to the rating to see if I can see any trend. It sounds like that I'm going into the direction of actually doing some regression analysis. So let's take a look. Again, in the data frame, I have all of these features, So this was the data frame that's result of my common expression table that I actually created. But now I'm actually plotting time with respect to the rating. So we're taking a look at, okay, what is that relationship? It sounds like that this needs a little bit of... More feature engineering so it needs to have some transformation because it's not actually showing a linear trend but if I was curious and I wanted to actually create a heat map I could actually look at the heat map of all of these values of how many How many movies did we have that had that were like 100 minutes and then the rating average rating was 5.9 so you can actually find out those values with this heat map. The next thing that I would do is quickly showing you the trend kind of similar thing, that if I'm looking at the time and I'm looking at the median rating, what's that trend? So as the time increases, is there any influence on the median rating or not? And the last thing is that I am doing, I am filling fancy and I will fit a linear regression to the plot that we had before so that we can kind of see if there is any trend or not. So I walk you through the entire process. I gave you a big data and I told you that there is a new technology that you should use and be comfortable to use SQL in order to actually run big queries on the datasets. Once you run all of those big queries, you can do also some of the operations as part of those queries. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: SQL Module Summary and Next Steps
children.summary: The lecture concluded with a summary of the SQL module, emphasizing its relationship with pandas and the shift to data frames for visualization and modeling. Students are reminded to complete one lab and one homework assignment related to SQL. A new module will be introduced next week, maintaining the weekly progression of topics.

children.title: Course Wrap-Up and Upcoming Modules
children.summary: The lecture concluded with a summary of the SQL module, highlighting its connection to pandas and the transition to data frames for visualization and modeling. Students are reminded to complete one lab and one homework assignment related to SQL. A new module will be introduced next week, continuing the weekly progression of topics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We spend a lecture and half actually seeing that. Then we saw that it actually connects really well with pandas. We ported everything into data frame. Then we went ahead and actually did a bunch of different visualization and modeling. That's kind of a wrap up. The next thing that after this, you would need to do is just evaluating your model, but you're all familiar with it. Okay, that was it today. We are going to come back next week with a new topic. As I promised every week, there is going to be a new module. So this was a wrap up for SQL. You're going to do one lab and one homework on it. You're going to have fun. And we're going to see what we have for you next week. Thank you for coming. good sometimes your kids come early I don't know you have midterm Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

7431959
WITH
WITH
good_action_movies AS (
table_name1 AS (
SELECT *
SELECT ...
FROM Title T JOIN Rating R ON T.tconst = R.tconst
WHERE genres LIKE '%Action%' AND averageRating > 7 AND numVotes > 5000
),
).
table_name2 AS (
prolific_actors AS (
SELECT ...
SELECT N.nconst, primaryName, COUNT(*) as numRoles
FROM Name N JOIN Principal P ON N.nconst = P.nconst
WHERE category = 'actor'
SELECT ...
GROUP BY N.nconst, primaryName
FROM
)
SELECT primaryTitle, primaryName, numRoles, ROUND(averageRating) AS rating
table_name1,
FROM good_action_movies m, prolific_actors a, principal p
table_name2, ...
WHERE p.tconst = m.tconst AND p.nconst = a.nconst
ORDER BY rating DESC, numRoles DESC
LIMIT 10
55

WITH
good_action_movies AS (
table_name1 AS (
SELECT *
SELECT ...
FROM Title T JOIN Rating R ON T.tconst = R.tconst
WHERE genres LIKE '%Action%' AND averageRating > 7 AND numVotes > 5000
),
).
table_name2 AS (
prolific_actors AS (
SELECT ...
SELECT N.nconst, primaryName, COUNT(*) as numRoles
FROM Name N JOIN Principal P ON N.nconst = P.nconst
WHERE category = 'actor'
SELECT ...
GROUP BY N.nconst, primaryName
FROM
)
SELECT primaryTitle, primaryName, numRoles, ROUND(averageRating) AS rating
table_name1,
FROM good_action_movies m, prolific_actors a, principal p
table_name2, ...
WHERE p.tconst = m.tconst AND p.nconst = a.nconst
ORDER BY rating DESC, numRoles DESC
LIMIT 10
